why: The closest prior work and the IM ablation baseline ReAct beats (71 vs 53 on ALFWorld); the embodied reason-while-acting precursor.

title: FEVER: a Large-scale Dataset for Fact Extraction and VERification
why: One of ReAct's two knowledge tasks; the canonical claim-verification benchmark for grounded fact-checking, widely reused across QA/eval papers.

title: Selection-Inference: Exploiting Large Language Models for Interpretable Logical Reasoning
why: DeepMind's modular two-step (selection→inference) reasoning architecture; a structured-reasoning sibling to the CoT lineage ReAct extends.

title: A Generalist Agent
why: DeepMind's Gato — a single transformer policy across hundreds of tasks; the multi-task generalist-agent reference the action-generation line ReAct contrasts with.

title: Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback
why: The direct sibling Reflexion contrasts with: single-output self-feedback refinement with no persistent cross-trial memory and no environment grounding.

title: HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face
why: Influential LLM-controller tool-orchestration agent (routes tasks to HF models); a major agent-paradigm hub alongside ReAct/Reflexion.

title: Language Models can Solve Computer Tasks
why: RCI (Recursive Criticize and Improve) — a self-critique computer-control agent, a close self-correction sibling to Reflexion's verbal feedback loop.

title: CodeRL: Mastering Code Generation through Pretrained Models and Deep Reinforcement Learning
why: Unit-test-feedback code generation trained with deep RL — a precursor to Reflexion's test-grounded code-repair loop.

title: REFINER: Reasoning Feedback on Intermediate Representations
why: Trains a critic to give feedback on intermediate reasoning steps — a structured-feedback refinement sibling to Reflexion's verbal self-reflection.

title: Reasoning with Language Model is Planning with World Model
why: Concurrent MCTS-for-reasoning framework (RAP) directly compared in ToT's related work — treats LM reasoning as planning with an internal world model; the Monte-Carlo-tree-search sibling of deliberate LM search.

title: Language Models as Zero-Shot Planners: Extracting Actionable Knowledge for Embodied Agents
why: Foundational result that LMs can decompose high-level goals into actionable step plans zero-shot; an early pillar of the LM-planning / embodied-agent lineage ToT builds on.

title: Plan-and-Solve Prompting: Improving Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought Reasoning by Large Language Models
why: Zero-shot 'plan then solve' prompting that improves chain-of-thought without exemplars; a reasoning-prompt sibling in the CoT→ToT family.

title: LLM+P: Empowering Large Language Models with Optimal Planning Proficiency
why: LLM+P delegates the actual planning to a classical PDDL planner — the neuro-symbolic counterpoint to ToT's in-LM search, cited in ToT's related work.

title: Describe, Explain, Plan and Select: Interactive Planning with Large Language Models Enables Open-World Multi-Task Agents
why: DEPS: an interactive describe-explain-plan-select agent (Minecraft) that self-explains failures and selects sub-goals; an embodied multi-step planning sibling in ToT's planning-and-decision-making cluster.

title: AGIEval: A Human-Centric Benchmark for Evaluating Foundation Models
why: Human-exam-derived foundation-model benchmark; the knowledge-eval lineage MCP-RADAR positions tool-use evaluation against.

title: Chameleon: Plug-and-Play Compositional Reasoning with Large Language Models
why: Plug-and-play tool-composition framework; a foundational tool-orchestration ancestor of the MCP paradigm MCP-RADAR evaluates.

title: ToRA: A Tool-Integrated Reasoning Agent for Mathematical Problem Solving
why: Tool-integrated reasoning agent for math; the ToRA line MCP-RADAR adapts its hardest Math tasks from.

title: TALM: Tool Augmented Language Models
why: One of the earliest tool-augmented LM formulations; a root node of the tool-use lineage the MCP cluster extends.

title: Tool Documentation Enables Zero-Shot Tool-Usage with Large Language Models
why: Shows tool documentation can replace demonstrations for tool use — the direct evidence behind MCP-RADAR's 'optimize tool descriptions' recommendation to MCP developers.

title: Benchmarking Cognitive Biases in Large Language Models as Evaluators
why: CoBBLEr — extends FairEval's positional bias into a six-bias taxonomy of LLM-evaluator cognitive biases; the direct descendant generalizing 'judges aren't fair' across bias types.

title: LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment
why: The Superficial Alignment Hypothesis — near-frontier responses from ~1k curated examples; a foundational alignment node and one of the GPT-4-evaluated systems in FairEval's reference lineage.

title: BERTScore: Evaluating Text Generation with BERT
why: The BERT-embedding text-generation metric the LLM-as-judge era displaced; FairEval cites it as the prior automated-eval paradigm insufficient for measuring alignment with human intent.

title: Principle-Driven Self-Alignment of Language Models from Scratch with Minimal Human Supervision
why: Dromedary — self-alignment from a small set of human-written principles with minimal supervision; an early rules/principles-as-supervision ancestor of the rubric-reward line, cited by FairEval among LLM-evaluation works.

title: The Perils of Using Mechanical Turk to Evaluate Open-Ended Text Generation
why: The motivating prior work: AMT crowds can't distinguish GPT-2 from human-written stories while trained English teachers can — the result Chiang & Lee's whole 'hire teachers, not Turkers' design rests on.

title: ChatGPT Outperforms Crowd-Workers for Text-Annotation Tasks
why: Concurrent landmark (Chiang & Lee's novelty footnote): ChatGPT beats crowd-workers on text-annotation accuracy and agreement at ~1/30th the cost — the annotation-side companion to LLM-as-evaluator.

title: All That's 'Human' Is Not Gold: Evaluating Human Evaluation of Generated Text
why: Foundational critique that even untrained humans can't reliably tell machine- from human-written text — motivates both stronger human eval and the LLM-eval alternative.

title: Is ChatGPT a Good NLG Evaluator? A Preliminary Study
why: Concurrent study (cited in Chiang & Lee's footnote) testing ChatGPT as an automatic NLG metric across summarization, translation, and dialogue — a direct sibling LLM-as-evaluator probe.

title: GPTScore: Evaluate as You Desire
why: Foundational generative LLM-as-evaluator: score text by the conditional probability a pretrained LM assigns it given an instruction and aspect definition — the probability-based sibling to G-Eval and Chiang & Lee's prompt-based rating.

title: Improving alignment of dialogue agents via targeted human judgements
why: DeepMind's Sparrow — RLHF dialogue agent with rule-based judgements; a foundational alignment ancestor linking to Constitutional AI and Rule-Based Rewards.

title: Self-critiquing models for assisting human evaluators
why: OpenAI scalable-oversight result — models write critiques that help humans judge outputs; an LLM-as-judge / oversight precursor.

title: Quark: Controllable Text Generation with Reinforced Unlearning
why: One of AlpacaFarm's six reference LPF methods — reward-binned RL for controllable generation; a direct algorithmic node.

title: Super-NaturalInstructions: Generalization via Declarative Instructions on 1600+ NLP Tasks
why: The canonical 1,600-task instruction-following benchmark; the instruction-generalization lineage the Alpaca/SFT line builds on.

title: The Flan Collection: Designing Data and Methods for Effective Instruction Tuning
why: The definitive study of instruction-tuning data mixtures and methods (Flan 2022); foundational to the SFT step every LPF method starts from.

title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
why: RoPE — the rotary positional embedding LLaMA adopts and now near-universal across open LLMs; a major architecture hub connecting most modern models.

title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
why: 4-bit + LoRA finetuning that made tuning LLaMA-class models feasible on a single GPU; the efficient-finetuning hub of the open-weights era.

title: GLU Variants Improve Transformer
why: Introduces SwiGLU, the gated activation LLaMA uses in place of ReLU; with RoPE and RMSNorm completes the modern Transformer-block recipe.

title: Root Mean Square Layer Normalization
why: RMSNorm — the pre-normalization LLaMA applies to each sub-layer input; the third leg (with RoPE and SwiGLU) of the now-standard open-LLM architecture.

title: Stanford Alpaca: An Instruction-following LLaMA Model
why: The 52K-Self-Instruct fine-tune of LLaMA-7B that made instruction-following open and cheap; the spark of the Alpaca/Vicuna open-tuning wave the MT-Bench cast belongs to.

title: BART: Denoising Sequence-to-Sequence Pre-training for Natural Language Generation, Translation, and Comprehension
why: Denoising seq2seq pretraining (a LexGLUE-cited SOTA model); a ~13k-cite encoder-decoder hub bridging BERT and T5 in the pretraining lineage.

title: DeBERTa: Decoding-enhanced BERT with Disentangled Attention
why: Disentangled-attention encoder that improved on BERT/RoBERTa; a LexGLUE baseline and a foundational NLU architecture node alongside BERT/RoBERTa/T5.

title: Big Bird: Transformers for Longer Sequences
why: Sparse-attention long-document Transformer (global+window+random); a LexGLUE long-text baseline and the long-context sibling of Longformer/FlashAttention.

title: AI and the Everything in the Whole Wide World Benchmark
why: Construct-validity critique arguing 'general' benchmarks can't measure general ability; the eval-rigor touchstone (LexGLUE cites it) for the atlas's benchmark-skepticism lens.

title: What Will it Take to Fix Benchmarking in Natural Language Understanding?
authors: Samuel R. Bowman, George E. Dahl
why: Bowman & Dahl's four criteria for trustworthy NLU benchmarks; the methodology critique LexGLUE's design cites, linking to the scalable-oversight/eval-rigor cluster.

title: ContractNLI: A Dataset for Document-level Natural Language Inference for Contracts
why: Document-level NLI over contracts (NDAs) — does the contract entail/contradict a hypothesis about a clause; a direct contract-review sibling to CUAD's span extraction, and a frequent CUAD co-benchmark.

title: Pile of Law: Learning Responsible Data Filtering from the Law and a 256GB Open-Source Legal Dataset
why: A 256GB open legal corpus with responsible-data-filtering lessons drawn from the law; the foundational legal-domain pretraining data from the Stanford RegLab group behind LegalBench.

title: LegalBench-RAG: A Benchmark for Retrieval-Augmented Generation in the Legal Domain
why: A legal-domain RAG retrieval benchmark built directly on CUAD, MAUD and others; bridges the legal-NLP cluster to the RAG-eval line (FinanceBench, Contriever, Lost-in-the-Middle).

title: MAUD: An Expert-Annotated Legal NLP Dataset for Merger Agreement Understanding
why: The Atticus Project's merger-agreement counterpart to CUAD — expert-annotated multiple-choice reading comprehension over M&A contracts; the direct CUAD sibling in the expert-legal-dataset lineage.

title: ContractEval: Benchmarking LLMs for Clause-Level Legal Risk Identification in Commercial Contracts
why: Benchmarks frontier LLMs on clause-level legal-risk identification in commercial contracts; the LLM-era successor that revisits CUAD's needle-in-a-contract task with modern models.

title: Language Models as Knowledge Bases?
why: The LAMA probe — tests how much factual knowledge a pre-trained LM stores in its weights as a 'parametric knowledge base'; the parametric-memory framing (co-authored by RAG's Petroni) RAG sets out to augment.

title: Reading Wikipedia to Answer Open-Domain Questions
why: DrQA — the seminal 'retrieve Wikipedia + read' open-domain QA pipeline; the retriever-reader lineage RAG generalizes from extraction to generation.

title: How Much Knowledge Can You Pack into the Parameters of a Language Model?
why: Closed-book QA — how much QA you can do with parametric memory alone (the T5+SSM line); the parametric-only baseline RAG's open-book retrieval beats, and a direct link to the T5 node.

title: Latent Retrieval for Weakly Supervised Open Domain Question Answering
why: ORQA — latent-retrieval open-domain QA with an Inverse Cloze Task; one of the two retrieval-augmented predecessors (with REALM) RAG explicitly builds its latent-document objective on.

title: Generalization through Memorization: Nearest Neighbor Language Models
why: kNN-LM — augments a language model with a nearest-neighbor datastore over training text; the other major non-parametric-memory line, a sibling to RAG's retrieve-and-generate memory.

title: Spider: A Large-Scale Human-Labeled Dataset for Complex and Cross-Domain Semantic Parsing and Text-to-SQL Task
why: The canonical large-scale, cross-domain text-to-SQL semantic-parsing benchmark; the structured-data-QA lineage FinQA's executable-program approach descends from.

title: MathQA: Towards Interpretable Math Word Problem Solving with Operation-Based Formalisms
why: Math word problems annotated with operation-based program formalisms (the NeRd lineage FinQA benchmarks against); the operation-program-as-reasoning predecessor to FinQA's DSL.

title: TabFact: A Large-scale Dataset for Table-based Fact Verification
why: Large-scale table-based fact verification from the same group (W. Chen); a foundational table-reasoning benchmark that anchors the table-QA subgraph FinQA sits in.

title: HybridQA: A Dataset of Multi-Hop Question Answering over Tabular and Textual Data
why: Multi-hop QA over both tables and text; the heterogeneous table+text QA predecessor whose setting FinQA brings into finance with multi-step numerical reasoning.

title: BloombergGPT: A Large Language Model for Finance
why: The landmark domain-specific finance LLM (50B, trained on a 363B-token financial corpus); the closed finance-model the open-model landscape FinBen evaluates was built to answer.

title: PIXIU: A Large Language Model, Instruction Data and Evaluation Benchmark for Finance
why: FinBen's direct predecessor from the same group: the FLARE benchmark plus the FinMA instruction-tuned model that FinBen reuses and extends from NLP-only tasks to seven aspects.

title: FinBERT: Financial Sentiment Analysis with Pre-trained Language Models
why: The foundational pre-LLM financial-sentiment model; the domain-adapted BERT baseline the finance-tuned LLMs in FinBen (FinMA, FinGPT) descend from.

title: Good debt or bad debt: Detecting semantic orientations in economic text
why: The Financial PhraseBank (FPB) — the canonical financial-sentiment dataset and one of FinBen's core textual-analysis tasks where FinMA-7B tops GPT-4 (F1 0.88).

title: TAT-QA: A Question Answering Benchmark on a Hybrid of Tabular and Textual Content in Finance
why: Numerical QA over hybrid table+text financial reports; the sibling of FinQA/ConvFinQA in FinBen's QA aspect and a core financial table-reasoning dataset.

title: JudgeLM: Fine-tuned Large Language Models are Scalable Judges
why: A dedicated fine-tuned judge model (used as one of the 13 judges in Judging the Judges); the train-a-judge alternative to prompting GPT-4.

title: LLMs instead of Human Judges? A Large Scale Empirical Study across 20 NLP Evaluation Tasks
why: The closest sibling meta-eval — 20 NLP tasks, 11 judge models against human labels — concurrent with Judging the Judges and reaching the same caution.

title: Who Validates the Validators? Aligning LLM-Assisted Evaluation of LLM Outputs with Human Preferences
why: EvalGen — interactively aligns LLM-assisted evaluation criteria with human judgement; the criteria-drift problem behind rubric and judge reliability.

title: Generative Judge for Evaluating Alignment
why: Auto-J — a 13B open generative judge that outputs both a verdict and a critique; an open-weights judge baseline for the LLM-as-judge cluster.

title: RLAIF vs. RLHF: Scaling Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with AI Feedback
why: The canonical replace-human-feedback-with-AI-feedback result; the self-evaluation-as-supervision setting whose self-preference risk this paper exposes.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models at Evaluating Instruction Following
why: LLMBar — a meta-benchmark testing whether LLM judges can be fooled; the judge-reliability lineage self-preference bias sits within.

title: Large Language Models Sensitivity to The Order of Options in Multiple-Choice Questions
why: The ordering-bias result this paper corrects for by swapping options; a core LLM-as-judge methodological confound.

title: Taken out of context: On measuring situational awareness in LLMs
why: Situational-awareness measurement in LLMs; self-recognition is a special case, framing the safety stakes of models knowing their own outputs.

title: LLMs as Narcissistic Evaluators: When Ego Inflates Evaluation Scores
why: The closest sibling study — self-preference bias in summarization across BERT/T5/GPT-3.5 — that this paper extends with a self-recognition mechanism.

title: Experimental Evidence on the Productivity Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence
why: Landmark RCT — ChatGPT cut mid-level professional writing time ~40% and raised quality, compressing the skill gap; the writing-task sibling to Generative AI at Work.

title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
why: The GitHub Copilot RCT — developers finished an HTTP-server task ~56% faster with Copilot; the canonical coding-productivity field experiment cited alongside this paper.

title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
why: The BCG x GPT-4 consultant experiment — large gains inside the 'jagged frontier' but harm outside it; the knowledge-work counterpart to Generative AI at Work.

title: When combinations of humans and AI are useful: A systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: Michelle Vaccaro, Abdullah Almaatouq, Thomas Malone
why: Meta-analysis of 100+ studies finding human-AI teams often underperform the best of human-or-AI alone — the complementarity caution this paper cites as motivation.

title: AI Assistance in Legal Analysis: An Empirical Study
why: Law-student experiment where AI assistance helped lower-skill students most — the legal-domain instance of generative AI compressing the skill distribution.

title: The future of employment: How susceptible are jobs to computerisation?
authors: Carl Benedikt Frey, Michael A. Osborne
why: The canonical automation-exposure study (47% of US jobs at risk) whose occupation-level probabilistic framing GPTs-are-GPTs inverts at the task level — the baseline the labor cluster is defined against.

title: What can machine learning do? Workforce implications
why: The 'Suitability for Machine Learning' rubric (co-authored by Daniel Rock) that is the direct methodological predecessor to the E0/E1/E2 LLM-exposure rubric.

title: The Skill Content of Recent Technological Change: An Empirical Exploration
authors: David H. Autor, Frank Levy, Richard J. Murnane
why: The task-based framework (routine vs non-routine) underlying every O*NET-task analysis of technology exposure — the theoretical root of the labor cluster.

title: The Race between Man and Machine: Implications of Technology for Growth, Factor Shares, and Employment
why: The task-automation/new-tasks growth model that formalizes how automation displaces and reinstates labor — the economic theory behind 'exposure is not destiny'.

title: Taxonomy of Risks posed by Language Models
why: DeepMind's six-area harm taxonomy for LLMs; a bridge from the labor-impact cluster into the safety/red-teaming cluster and a widely-cited risk-framing hub.

title: Long-form factuality in large language models
why: LongFact/SAFE — the long-form factuality counterpart from the same lineage that directly addresses SimpleQA's stated main limitation (does short-form factuality generalize to long-form?).

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
why: The canonical long-form factuality metric (decompose into atomic facts, score each) — the highest-cited factuality-eval node SimpleQA's short-form design is explicitly a reaction to.

title: Teaching Models to Express Their Uncertainty in Words
why: Verbalized-confidence calibration — fine-tunes a model to state calibrated uncertainty in words; the methods-side counterpart to SimpleQA's stated-confidence calibration measurement.

title: FreshLLMs: Refreshing Large Language Models with Search Engine Augmentation
why: FreshQA — a QA benchmark for fast-changing knowledge; the time-varying sibling that SimpleQA's deliberately evergreen, time-stable question design contrasts with.

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
why: A large-scale hallucination-evaluation benchmark; the hallucination-detection node in SimpleQA's factuality cluster.

title: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models
why: Atlas — the retrieval-augmented LM Toolformer uses as its question-answering tool; bridges the tool-use cluster to the RAG/Contriever retrieval lineage.

title: MM-REACT: Prompting ChatGPT for Multimodal Reasoning and Action
why: Multimodal ReAct-style orchestration that prompts ChatGPT to call vision experts; extends the reasoning-and-acting/tool-use line into images and video.

title: Unnatural Instructions: Tuning Language Models with (Almost) No Human Labor
why: Same-group synthetic-instruction-data method in the self-annotation lineage Toolformer builds on — generate a tuning set with almost no human labels.

title: ToolkenGPT: Augmenting Frozen Language Models with Massive Tools via Tool Embeddings
why: Augments a frozen LM with thousands of tools via learned 'toolken' embeddings — a scalable alternative to Toolformer's finetune-on-API-calls recipe.

title: ART: Automatic multi-step reasoning and tool-use for large language models
why: Interleaves chain-of-thought with tool calls drawn from a task library at inference — the prompting-side counterpart to Toolformer's finetuning approach.

title: Constructing A Multi-hop QA Dataset for Comprehensive Evaluation of Reasoning Steps
why: 2WikiMultiHopQA — the sibling multi-hop benchmark that adds evidence/reasoning-path annotations to attack HotpotQA's shortcut problem; a standard multi-hop-QA pair-partner in retrieval-agent evals.

title: MuSiQue: Multihop Questions via Single-hop Question Composition
why: Builds 2–4 hop questions by composing single-hop ones specifically to remove the reasoning shortcuts that made HotpotQA answerable single-hop — the 'multi-hop done right' rebuttal to HotpotQA's central critique.

title: Interleaving Retrieval with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Knowledge-Intensive Multi-Step Questions
why: IRCoT — interleaves retrieval with chain-of-thought for multi-hop QA over HotpotQA/2Wiki/MuSiQue; a foundational retrieval-augmented reasoning method bridging the QA-benchmark line to the ReAct agentic-retrieval cluster.

title: Compositional Questions Do Not Necessitate Multi-hop Reasoning
why: The shortcut critique: shows a single-hop BERT model answers a large share of HotpotQA, proving 'written over two paragraphs' ≠ 'requires two hops' — the eval-rigor lesson the atlas's critique lens is built around.

title: Avoiding Reasoning Shortcuts: Adversarial Evaluation, Training, and Model Development for Multi-Hop QA
why: Builds adversarial distractors that crater HotpotQA scores, exposing how much the distractor setting rewards topical shortcuts over reasoning — the empirical case for adversarial-distractor benchmark design.

title: World of Bits: An Open-Domain Platform for Web-Based Agents
why: The MiniWoB platform — the foundational web-agent RL benchmark and root of the lineage (WebShop -> WebArena -> WebVoyager -> OSWorld) the atlas's web-agent cluster descends from.

title: Language Agent Tree Search Unifies Reasoning, Acting, and Planning in Language Models
why: LATS marries MCTS to ReAct-style reasoning+acting (evaluated on WebShop, HotPotQA, HumanEval); ties the ReAct/ToT/Reflexion methods cluster together.

title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents
why: Fine-grained, progress-rate eval board for multi-turn LLM agents (incl. WebShop) — directly on-theme for the atlas's eval-rigor lens on agent benchmarks.

title: ExpeL: LLM Agents Are Experiential Learners
why: Agents that distill experience into natural-language insights without gradient updates (evaluated on WebShop/ALFWorld/HotPotQA); a high-cite memory-augmented-agent node for the Reflexion cluster.

title: AgentTuning: Enabling Generalized Agent Abilities for LLMs
why: Instruction-tunes open LLMs on agent trajectories (AgentInstruct, incl. WebShop tasks) from the AgentBench/Tsinghua group; the agent-SFT counterpart to the agent-eval cluster.

title: Search-o1: Agentic Search-Enhanced Large Reasoning Models
why: The direct predecessor by the same lead author and the headline open-source baseline WebThinker beats — agentic RAG with a Reason-in-Documents module that merges retrieval into o1-style reasoning via prompting.

title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
why: The canonical adaptive-RAG method: a model that decides when to retrieve and critiques its own outputs with reflection tokens — the highest-cited RAG-workflow node WebThinker's autonomous search supersedes.

title: Search-R1: Training LLMs to Reason and Leverage Search Engines with Reinforcement Learning
why: Trains an LLM to interleave reasoning and search-engine calls from scratch with RL — the dominant 'RL to search' line (1,100+ cites) WebThinker's iterative-DPO tool training sits alongside.

title: ReTool: Reinforcement Learning for Strategic Tool Use in LLMs
why: RL that teaches models when and how to invoke tools (code interpreter) during reasoning — a tool-use-RL sibling to WebThinker's research-tool DPO.

title: ReSearch: Learning to Reason with Search for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
why: Treats search as part of the reasoning chain and optimizes the whole trajectory with RL (no step-level supervision) — a concurrent search-as-reasoning-RL method in WebThinker's reference cluster.

title: R1-Searcher: Incentivizing the Search Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
why: The RAG-based RL search baseline DeepResearcher directly outperforms; a Wikipedia-confined RL-search agent and the closest contrast to real-web training.

title: ZeroSearch: Incentivize the Search Capability of LLMs without Searching
why: The opposite design philosophy: trains search ability against an LLM-simulated search engine with no real API calls; a direct counterpoint to DeepResearcher's live-web RL.

title: Measuring and Narrowing the Compositionality Gap in Language Models
why: Introduces Self-Ask and the Bamboogle benchmark (the OOD set where DeepResearcher's real-web edge is clearest); foundational multi-hop reasoning-via-search node.

title: OWL: Optimized Workforce Learning for General Multi-Agent Assistance in Real-World Task Automation
why: An open multi-agent assistance framework cited as a prompt-engineered deep-research system; a hierarchical multi-agent baseline contrast.

title: Open Deep Search: Democratizing Search with Open-source Reasoning Agents
why: An open-source deep-search reasoning agent (prompt/workflow-based) positioned as the open alternative to commercial Deep Research; a sibling system to DeepResearcher.

title: DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines
why: The declarative LM-programming framework STORM (and much of the agent/eval tooling in this atlas) is built on; foundational pipeline infrastructure.

title: Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations
why: ALCE — the citation recall/precision definitions STORM directly adopts to score attribution; cornerstone of grounded-generation evaluation.

title: Prometheus: Inducing Fine-grained Evaluation Capability in Language Models
why: Open 13B rubric-based LLM evaluator STORM uses to grade articles; a key LLM-as-judge node for the rubric/eval cluster.

title: Into the Unknown Unknowns: Engaged Human Learning through Participation in Language Model Agent Conversations
why: Co-STORM — the same Stanford group's collaborative follow-up that turns STORM into an interactive human-in-the-loop research conversation.

title: Evaluating Frontier Models for Dangerous Capabilities
why: DeepMind's foundational dangerous-capability eval suite (persuasion, cyber, self-proliferation, CBRN reasoning); the risk-assessment lineage the time-horizon metric feeds and a hub for the FORTRESS / PropensityBench / biorisk safety cluster.

title: Forecasting Frontier Language Model Agent Capabilities
why: Directly forecasts agent benchmark performance ahead of model release — the capability-forecasting sibling to METR's time-horizon extrapolation.

title: xbench: Tracking Agents Productivity Scaling with Profession-Aligned Real-World Evaluations
why: Profession-aligned real-world agent evaluation that tracks productivity scaling over time — a direct companion to the time-horizon trend in the economic-automation cluster (RLI / GDPval / SWE-Lancer).

title: Scaling Long-Horizon LLM Agent via Context-Folding
why: A method for extending agents to long-horizon tasks via context folding — the capability-side counterpart to the long-task axis the time-horizon paper measures.

title: Kosmos: An AI Scientist for Autonomous Discovery
why: An autonomous AI-scientist system for end-to-end discovery; a frontier long-horizon research agent citing the time-horizon trend, bridging to the AI-co-scientist / PaperBench cluster.

title: How Much Does AI Impact Development Speed? An Enterprise-Based Randomized Controlled Trial
why: Google enterprise RCT finding a ~21% speedup from AI on developer tasks — the closest methodological sibling (an RCT with a fixed outcome) whose positive result becker contrasts with its slowdown.

title: The Effects of Generative AI on High-Skilled Work: Evidence from Three Field Experiments with Software Developers
why: Three field experiments (Microsoft, Accenture, a Fortune-100 firm) reporting ~26% more completed tasks with AI for experienced developers — a direct Table-3 comparison row to becker on non-synthetic, high-skill work.

title: Significant Productivity Gains through Programming with Large Language Models
why: Lab study reporting a 65% increase in the rate of task requirements satisfied with LLM assistance — one of the strong synthetic-task speedups becker argues do not generalize to real, in-context work.

title: CodeElo: Benchmarking Competition-level Code Generation of LLMs with Human-comparable Elo Ratings
why: A competition-programming benchmark giving LLMs human-comparable Elo ratings — the impressive-coding-benchmark evidence (alongside SWE-bench) whose apparent capability becker shows does not translate into real-world developer speedup.

title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery
why: The canonical fully-automated 'AI Scientist' (idea->experiment->paper->LLM review); Si et al. directly critique its LLM-reviewer (43.3% agreement).

title: ResearchAgent: Iterative Research Idea Generation over Scientific Literature with Large Language Models
why: Direct sibling: iterative LLM idea generation grounded in the literature; one of the small-reviewer ideation systems Si et al. measure against.

title: SciMON: Scientific Inspiration Machines Optimized for Novelty
why: Explicitly optimizes generated ideas for novelty against prior work — the closest precursor to Si et al.'s novelty-centric ideation question.

title: Large Language Models for Automated Open-domain Scientific Hypotheses Discovery
why: Open-domain hypothesis generation/discovery; another constrained-evaluation ideation system Si et al. contrast their large human study with.

title: DiscoveryBench: Towards Data-Driven Discovery with Large Language Models
why: A benchmark for data-driven scientific discovery; the evaluation counterpart in the AI-for-science cluster anchored by Si et al.

title: KernelBench: Can LLMs Write Efficient GPU Kernels?
why: One of the ten benchmarks ABC audits — its fuzzer never varies tensor shapes, overstating kernel-correctness by 31%; a core code/GPU agentic benchmark.

title: Rigor, Reliability, and Reproducibility Matter: A Decade-Scale Survey of 572 Code Benchmarks
why: The 572-code-benchmark methodology survey ABC builds its checklist formulation on — directly on-theme for the atlas's eval-rigor lens.

title: Can We Trust AI Benchmarks? An Interdisciplinary Review of Current Issues in AI Evaluation
why: An interdisciplinary review of pervasive validity/contamination issues in AI evaluation — the broad critique companion to ABC's concrete checklist.

title: Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
why: The comprehensive survey of agent-evaluation methods that cites ABC; a high-cite map of the agentic-eval landscape this atlas charts.

title: ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search via Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT
why: BEIR's late-interaction co-winner — token-level MaxSim scoring generalizes out of domain far better than dense bi-encoders (beats BM25 on 9/18 BEIR datasets); the architecture behind ColBERTv2/PLAID.

title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Negative Contrastive Learning for Dense Text Retrieval
why: ANCE — the MS-MARCO-trained dense bi-encoder BEIR shows fails to generalize out of domain; foundational hard-negative training (ANN-mined negatives) for dense retrieval.

title: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks
why: The Wikipedia-only knowledge-intensive benchmark (5 tasks over one KB) BEIR positions itself against; a precursor to heterogeneous, multi-domain retrieval evaluation.

title: Large Dual Encoders Are Generalizable Retrievers
why: GTR — the direct answer to BEIR's generalization gap: scaling T5-based dual encoders finally lets dense retrievers beat BM25 zero-shot on BEIR, evaluated head-to-head on the benchmark.

title: SPLADE: Sparse Lexical and Expansion Model for First Stage Ranking
authors: Thibault Formal, Benjamin Piwowarski, Stephane Clinchant
source: Lit search: neural sparse retrieval (BEIR cluster)
why: Learned sparse lexical-and-expansion retrieval — the modern descendant of BEIR's docT5query/SPARTA line, pairing learned term weighting with an inverted index for efficient first-stage ranking.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey
why: The canonical RAG survey (naive/advanced/modular RAG taxonomy); the field-defining reference for the retrieval-augmentation lineage GraphRAG extends.

title: RAPTOR: Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval
why: The closest sibling and the missing GraphRAG baseline: also builds a hierarchy of recursive summaries for retrieval, but via clustering a summary tree rather than LLM-graph community detection.

title: RAGAs: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
why: Reference-free automated RAG metrics (faithfulness, answer/context relevance); GraphRAG cites these as the vector-RAG criteria it argues miss global sensemaking quality.

title: MultiHop-RAG: Benchmarking Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multi-Hop Queries
why: A multi-hop RAG benchmark and the source of GraphRAG's News-article evaluation corpus; tests retrieval+reasoning across documents.

title: Knowledge Graph Prompting for Multi-Document Question Answering
why: A knowledge-graph-as-index RAG method for multi-document QA; the graph-traversal-at-query-time approach GraphRAG contrasts its index-time community summarization against.

title: OmniACT: A Dataset and Benchmark for Enabling Multimodal Generalist Autonomous Agents for Desktop and Web
why: The desktop+web action-script benchmark UGround evaluates on (9,802 tasks, 38 apps); the DetACT OCR/icon pipeline SeeAct-V replaces with pure vision grounding.

title: On the Effects of Data Scale on UI Control Agents
why: AndroidControl - the 15K-task Android dataset UGround both trains on and evaluates against; a key data-scaling study for computer-control agents.

title: OmniParser for Pure Vision Based GUI Agent
why: Microsoft's screenshot-parsing model that extracts interactable icons/text - the detect-then-act vision alternative to UGround's end-to-end coordinate grounding.

title: Ferret-UI 2: Mastering Universal User Interface Understanding Across Platforms
why: Apple's cross-platform UI-understanding MLLM; the direct universal-grounding peer to UGround released the same month.

title: WebCanvas: Benchmarking Web Agents in Online Environments
why: Defines Mind2Web-Live with key-node functional evaluation on live sites - the online web benchmark UGround reports completion rate and success rate on.

title: Qwen2-VL: Enhancing Vision-Language Model’s Perception of the World at Any Resolution
why: The open VLM backbone behind OS-Atlas-7B (and UGround); the dominant open multimodal foundation and a major hub node.

title: How far are we to GPT-4V? Closing the gap to commercial multimodal models with open-source suites
why: InternVL-1.5 — the open VLM family behind OS-Atlas-4B; a high-cite open multimodal backbone.

title: OS-Copilot: Towards Generalist Computer Agents with Self-Improvement
why: The generalist-computer-agent predecessor from the same lead author; conceptual ancestor of the OS-Atlas action-model line.

title: Ferret-UI: Grounded Mobile UI Understanding with Multimodal LLMs
why: Apple’s grounded mobile-UI understanding model; a key GUI-grounding baseline OS-Atlas compares its corpus against (Table 1).

title: GUI Odyssey: A Comprehensive Dataset for Cross-App GUI Navigation on Mobile Devices
why: Cross-app mobile GUI navigation benchmark used as a zero-shot OOD agent eval in OS-Atlas (GPT-4o 5.36 → OS-Atlas-7B 26.96 SR).

title: Android in the Wild: A Large-Scale Dataset for Android Device Control
why: AitW — the 715k-episode Android device-control dataset from AndroidWorld's own lead author; the static-demonstration mobile-agent corpus AndroidWorld's online, reward-based evaluation was built to move beyond.

title: Reinforcement Learning on Web Interfaces Using Workflow-Guided Exploration
why: The MiniWoB++ benchmark — the parameterized web-interface RL environment whose infinite task-variation design AndroidWorld models its per-seed parameterization on, and which it ports in as MobileMiniWoB++.

title: ALFRED: A Benchmark for Interpreting Grounded Instructions for Everyday Tasks
why: High-impact benchmark for interpreting grounded natural-language instructions for everyday household tasks; the embodied instruction-following lineage behind execution-graded agent evaluation.

title: Mapping Natural Language Instructions to Mobile UI Action Sequences
why: PixelHelp — a foundational Google dataset grounding NL instructions into mobile-UI action sequences, listed in AndroidWorld's environment comparison (Table 1).

title: UFO: A UI-Focused Agent for Windows OS Interaction
why: Microsoft's UI-focused dual-agent for Windows OS interaction; the Windows computer-use sibling to AndroidWorld in the OS-agent cluster.

title: Self-Rewarding Language Models
why: The model judges its own outputs to generate its own preference labels for iterative DPO — CAI's self-grading taken to its logical end, dropping even the fixed constitution.

title: SALMON: Self-Alignment with Instructable Reward Models
why: A reward model steered at inference by natural-language principles, so one RM scores any instruction — a direct descendant of CAI's principle-conditioned AI feedback.

title: Large Language Models Can Self-Improve
why: A pre-CAI self-improvement result (generate, filter by self-consistency, finetune) that CAI cites; the self-bootstrapping sibling to SL-CAI's critique-revise loop.

title: Supervising strong learners by amplifying weak experts
why: Iterated Amplification — the foundational scalable-oversight proposal CAI invokes; anchors the oversight subgraph alongside AI Safety via Debate and Weak-to-Strong.

title: Training Language Models with Language Feedback
why: Learning from written natural-language feedback rather than scalar preferences — CAI calls its method 'very similar to our supervised constitutional step'.

title: DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model
why: The direct predecessor that introduced Multi-head Latent Attention and DeepSeekMoE — the two architectural pillars V3 inherits wholesale.

title: DeepSeekMoE: Towards Ultimate Expert Specialization in Mixture-of-Experts Language Models
why: The fine-grained expert segmentation + shared-expert design behind V3's 1-shared / 256-routed MoE layers.

title: Auxiliary-Loss-Free Load Balancing Strategy for Mixture-of-Experts
why: The bias-term routing method V3 pioneers at scale — balancing experts without an auxiliary loss that degrades quality.

title: GShard: Scaling Giant Models with Conditional Computation and Automatic Sharding
why: The foundational sparse-MoE scaling paper (conditional computation + sharding) the entire MoE-LLM lineage V3 culminates builds on.

title: FP8-LM: Training FP8 Large Language Models
why: An FP8 mixed-precision training precedent; V3 claims the first validation of FP8 at extremely large scale and cites this line of work.

title: SimPO: Simple Preference Optimization with a Reference-Free Reward
why: Reference-free preference optimization (length-normalized reward, no reference model); a direct DPO sibling and a core node for the post-training cluster TÜLU 3's DPO stage sits in.

title: Zephyr: Direct Distillation of LM Alignment
why: Zephyr — direct distillation of LM alignment; the open post-training predecessor TÜLU 3 explicitly names and surpasses, anchoring the open-recipe lineage.

title: UltraFeedback: Boosting Language Models with High-quality Feedback
why: UltraFeedback — the high-quality AI-feedback preference dataset used to train TÜLU 3's general reward model; a central preference-data connector.

title: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval: A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
why: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval — the exact AlpacaEval 2 LC-win-rate metric reported across every TÜLU 3 results table; debiases automatic chat evaluation for length.

title: OLMo: Accelerating the Science of Language Models
venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
why: OLMo — Ai2's fully-open base-model effort; the open-science sibling to TÜLU 3 (open data + code + recipe) and another Ai2 anchor node.

title: BeaverTails: Towards Improved Safety Alignment of LLM via a Human-Preference Dataset
why: Large human-preference safety dataset separating helpfulness from harmlessness; a core safety-RLHF data node RBR's human baseline lineage builds on.

title: Safe RLHF: Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
why: Decouples helpfulness and harmlessness as a constrained-RL problem; the safety-RL formulation RBR contrasts with by injecting rules directly into PPO.

title: A Holistic Approach to Undesired Content Detection in the Real World
why: OpenAI's Moderation API content classifier (ModAPI) — the moderation model RBR uses to curate safety-relevant prompts and as an external safety metric.

title: Specific versus General Principles for Constitutional AI
why: Anthropic study on how specific vs broad constitutional principles shape behavior — the specificity question RBR answers with fine-grained, composable rules.

title: Safety-Tuned LLaMAs: Lessons From Improving the Safety of Large Language Models that Follow Instructions
why: Shows small amounts of safety data trade off against over-refusal/exaggerated safety — the safety-vs-usefulness tension RBR is designed to rebalance.

title: KTO: Model Alignment as Prospect Theoretic Optimization
why: KTO replaces DPO's pairwise BT loss with a prospect-theory (Kahneman-Tversky) utility, learning from unpaired good/bad signals; a top DPO successor and common Tulu/post-training baseline.

title: A General Theoretical Paradigm to Understand Learning from Human Preferences
venue: International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics
why: DeepMind's IPO / general PsiPO framework that unifies RLHF and DPO and fixes DPO's overfitting to deterministic preferences; the key theoretical follow-up to DPO.

title: ORPO: Monolithic Preference Optimization without Reference Model
venue: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
why: ORPO folds preference alignment into SFT with an odds-ratio penalty, removing the separate reference model and DPO stage entirely; a monolithic single-stage alternative.

title: Is Reinforcement Learning (Not) for Natural Language Processing?: Benchmarks, Baselines, and Building Blocks for Natural Language Policy Optimization
why: RL4LMs/NLPO: the open RL-for-NLP benchmark and library DPO cites; anchors the 'is RL worth it for LMs' question DPO answers in the negative.

title: Aligning Language Models with Preferences through f-divergence Minimization
why: f-DPO generalizes the KL constraint to any f-divergence (reverse-KL, JSD, alpha) for preference fine-tuning; a direct DPO reference that broadens its divergence family.

title: PuzzleWorld: A Benchmark for Multimodal, Open-Ended Reasoning in Puzzlehunts
why: Direct multimodal puzzlehunt descendant with step-level reasoning annotations — grades where in the chain a model fails, not just EnigmaEval's final answer.

title: lmgame-Bench: How Good are LLMs at Playing Games?
why: Evaluates LLMs as agents on classic games (Tetris, Sokoban, 2048) with perception/memory/planning harnesses — a games-as-reasoning eval that cites EnigmaEval.

title: Sudoku-Bench: Evaluating creative reasoning with Sudoku variants
why: Sakana AI benchmark of novel Sudoku variants requiring fresh rule inference — the structured-puzzle counterpart to EnigmaEval's unstructured hunts.

title: PUZZLES: A Benchmark for Neural Algorithmic Reasoning
why: EnigmaEval's algorithmic-reasoning predecessor: 40 types of visual logic puzzles for RL agents; the consistent-format puzzle bench EnigmaEval contrasts its format diversity against.

title: Step-by-Step Reasoning to Solve Grid Puzzles: Where do LLMs Falter?
why: 274 grid puzzles (GridPuzzle) graded on the reasoning chain rather than the final answer — the step-level evaluation EnigmaEval's string match forgoes.

title: BetterBench: Assessing AI Benchmarks, Uncovering Issues, and Establishing Best Practices
why: A NeurIPS meta-evaluation of 24 AI benchmarks against a 46-criterion quality framework — the benchmark-quality lens CFPD's own construct-validity caveats sit within.

title: Escalation Risks from Language Models in Military and Diplomatic Decision-Making
why: The foundational LLM-in-wargames study showing models choose escalatory, even nuclear, actions — the crisis-simulation work CFPD turns into a reproducible benchmark; would connect directly to CFPD.

title: Exploring the Sensitivity of LLMs' Decision-Making Capabilities: Insights from Prompt Variations and Hyperparameters
why: Shows LLM course-of-action recommendations flip with minor prompt rewording — the prompt-sensitivity problem CFPD's two-reviewer neutrality checks are built to mitigate.

title: Measuring Free-Form Decision-Making Inconsistency of Language Models in Military Crisis Simulations
why: Quantifies how inconsistently LLMs decide across military crisis simulations — an adjacent national-security eval complementing CFPD's forced-choice design.

title: Open-Ended Wargames with Large Language Models
why: Uses LLMs to run open-ended (free-text) wargames — the qualitative crisis-simulation counterpart to CFPD's structured, codified-response benchmark.

title: Gemini 1.5: Unlocking multimodal understanding across millions of tokens of context
why: The frontier long-context multimodal model HLE evaluates; a heavily-cited foundation-model hub (3.7k cites) tying the eval nodes to the model side of the graph.

title: Know What You Don't Know: Unanswerable Questions for SQuAD
why: Added unanswerable questions to SQuAD to force abstention — the foundational reading-comprehension benchmark for the know-what-you-don't-know / calibration theme HLE's confidently-wrong finding revives.

title: AgentHarm: A Benchmark for Measuring Harmfulness of LLM Agents
why: A benchmark measuring how harmful LLM agents can be across malicious tasks; a safety-eval node bridging HLE's frontier-capability framing to the red-teaming cluster.

title: ARC Prize 2024: Technical Report
why: Chollet's ARC-AGI competition report on abstraction-and-reasoning tasks that resist memorization — a contamination-resistant frontier-reasoning benchmark HLE cites as a peer.

title: MMLU-Pro+: Evaluating Higher-Order Reasoning and Shortcut Learning in LLMs
why: A harder MMLU successor probing higher-order reasoning and penalizing shortcut learning — directly targets the MMLU saturation that motivates HLE.

title: LLMs Get Lost In Multi-Turn Conversation
why: The definitive multi-turn degradation study — an average 39% single→multi-turn drop across 200k+ simulated conversations; the highest-cited follow-up in MultiChallenge's lineage.

title: Scaling Synthetic Data Creation with 1,000,000,000 Personas
why: PersonaHub — the billion-persona collection MultiChallenge samples to diversify its synthetic conversations; an influential synthetic-data-generation method.

title: MT-Eval: A Multi-Turn Capabilities Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
why: The multi-turn capabilities benchmark MultiChallenge contrasts itself with — now near-saturated by frontier models, motivating a harder successor.

title: Are You Sure? Challenging LLMs Leads to Performance Drops in The FlipFlop Experiment
why: Shows LLMs flip correct answers when merely challenged — the sycophancy-under-pressure phenomenon MultiChallenge's Self-Coherence category operationalizes.

title: AgentInstruct: Toward Generative Teaching with Agentic Flows
why: Microsoft's multi-agent synthetic-data-generation framework — the agentic-flows lineage behind MultiChallenge's MMSE conversation generator.

title: Quantifying Memorization Across Neural Language Models
why: The memorization-quantification work GSM1k's per-character log-likelihood probe rests on — models assign higher likelihood to sequences they were trained on.

title: Leak, Cheat, Repeat: Data Contamination and Evaluation Malpractices in Closed-Source LLMs
why: A systematic audit of data contamination and evaluation malpractice across closed-source LLMs — the survey backdrop GSM1k tests empirically on GSM8k.

title: Functional Benchmarks for Robust Evaluation of Reasoning Performance, and the Reasoning Gap
why: The functional-evaluation approach (benchmarks as infinite problem generators) GSM1k directly contrasts with — also finds large contamination-driven reasoning gaps, including on MATH.

title: Detecting Pretraining Data from Large Language Models
why: Min-K% Prob membership inference — the pretraining-data-detection line for deciding whether a benchmark leaked, complementary to GSM1k's held-out-twin behavioral test.

title: Benchmarking Benchmark Leakage in Large Language Models
why: Proposes detection metrics (perplexity, n-gram accuracy) to quantify benchmark leakage during training — the leakage-measurement sibling to GSM1k's behavioral overfitting test.

title: ToolPRM: Fine-Grained Inference Scaling of Structured Outputs for Function Calling
why: Builds a process reward model for function-calling and uses it for inference-time scaling — the direct descendant of ToolComp's PRM-for-tool-use thesis.

title: FOFO: A Benchmark to Evaluate LLMs' Format-Following Capability
why: The format-following benchmark ToolComp explicitly defers formatting evaluation to; isolates instruction/format compliance from task correctness.

title: RealTime QA: What's the Answer Right Now?
why: A dynamic, time-sensitive QA benchmark requiring retrieval tools — foundational for evaluating tool-augmented models on answers that change over time.

title: TTPA: Token-level Tool-use Preference Alignment Training Framework with Fine-grained Evaluation
why: Aligns tool-use behavior at token granularity with fine-grained rewards — the post-training counterpart to ToolComp's process-supervision evaluation.

title: BigFinanceBench: A Workflow-Grounded Benchmark for Financial-Research Agents
why: A workflow-grounded benchmark for multi-step financial-research agents — extends ToolComp's verified-trajectory tool-use evaluation into a high-stakes professional domain.

title: AI deception: A survey of examples, risks, and potential solutions
why: The canonical survey of documented LLM deception MASK cites as motivation — catalogs real cases like GPT-4's TaskRabbit CAPTCHA ruse; frames the honesty problem MASK measures.

title: Inverse Scaling: When Bigger Isn't Better
why: The inverse-scaling competition documenting tasks where larger models do worse — empirical grounding for MASK's finding that scale buys accuracy but not honesty.

title: Safetywashing: Do AI Safety Benchmarks Actually Measure Safety Progress?
why: The CAIS paper that named 'safetywashing' (capability dressed up as safety); MASK's core critique of honesty benchmarks builds directly on it — same lead author (Ren).

title: Alignment for Honesty
why: Training methods to make models acknowledge what they don't know — the honesty-training counterpart to MASK's measurement of lying under pressure.

title: Utility Engineering: Analyzing and Controlling Emergent Value Systems in AIs
why: CAIS/Scale AI study of emergent value systems in LLMs (Mazeika, a MASK co-author); MASK uses its utility framework to test whether models' internal values undervalue honesty.

title: Universal Adversarial Triggers for Attacking and Analyzing NLP
why: Foundational trigger-based adversarial attack on NLP models; cited by Holistic T&E as an automated red-teaming technique and a precursor to GCG-style attacks.

title: Making Pre-trained Language Models Better Few-shot Learners
authors: Tianyu Gao, Adam Fisch, Danqi Chen
why: Highly-cited prompt-based few-shot fine-tuning (LM-BFF); cited for the in-context few-shot framing that motivates open-ended LLM evaluation.

title: garak: A Framework for Security Probing Large Language Models
why: The open-source vulnerability scanner used as the automated red-teaming layer in Holistic T&E; standard tooling for rule-based LLM security probes.

title: Taxonomy of Machine Learning Safety: A Survey and Primer
why: ML-safety taxonomy that Holistic T&E draws on to construct its LLM risk taxonomy; framing for the Safety & Red-teaming cluster.

title: HotFlip: White-Box Adversarial Examples for Text Classification
why: Early white-box gradient-based adversarial text attack; cited by Holistic T&E in its account of trigger/adversarial-prompt vulnerabilities.

title: SFT Memorizes, RL Generalizes: A Comparative Study of Foundation Model Post-training
why: SFT-memorizes vs RL-generalizes — the debate Guide's self-distillation-vs-capability-gain decomposition directly engages; highest-cited reference.

title: Learning to Reason under Off-Policy Guidance
why: The off-policy-guidance method Guide most directly builds on — reasoning under hints with an off-policy correction.

title: Beyond Human Data: Scaling Self-Training for Problem-Solving with Language Models
why: ReST-EM: the self-training/distillation lineage Guide's 'self-distillation dominates' framing sits within.

title: ReST-MCTS*: LLM Self-Training via Process Reward Guided Tree Search
why: Self-training via process-reward tree search — the pass@k->pass@1 expert-iteration line Guide extends.

title: Echo Chamber: RL Post-training Amplifies Behaviors Learned in Pretraining
why: Concurrent evidence that RL post-training re-allocates pretrained behaviors — corroborates Guide's 'RL mostly re-allocates probability mass' thesis.

title: Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction
why: Mechanistic account of refusal as one activation direction — the exact safeguard J2 exploits; explains why refusal training is brittle.

title: Representation Engineering: A Top-Down Approach to AI Transparency
why: RepE: reading/steering concepts (refusal, honesty) via representations; foundation for circuit-breaker defenses that J2-style attacks stress-test.

title: How Johnny Can Persuade LLMs to Jailbreak Them: Rethinking Persuasion to Challenge AI Safety by Humanizing LLMs
why: Persuasion-taxonomy jailbreak (PAP) used as a J2 baseline; the human-persuasion framing J2's attacker-making prompt mirrors.

title: Improving Alignment and Robustness with Circuit Breakers
why: The defense side — interrupts harmful internal representations; the kind of safeguard J2's willingness-to-jailbreak detour is meant to probe.

title: Jailbreaking Leading Safety-Aligned LLMs with Simple Adaptive Attacks
why: Strong adaptive black-box jailbreak baseline; complements J2's automated-attack comparison set.

title: A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge
why: The reference survey for the LM-as-judge paradigm APEX's single-judge grading rests on; anchors the graph's rubric/judge cluster (RaR, PRBench, MASK).

title: LMUnit: Fine-grained Evaluation with Natural Language Unit Tests
why: The direct 'rubric criteria = unit tests' grading ancestor (Saad-Falcon, Vidgen, Kiela) that APEX and the whole rubric cluster build on.

title: The Iceberg Index: Measuring Skills-centered Exposure in the AI Economy
why: Economy-wide skills-exposure measurement that cites APEX; pairs with the GDPval / RLI / UpBench economic-impact cluster.

title: KWBench: Measuring Unprompted Problem Recognition in Knowledge Work
why: Knowledge-work benchmark (cites APEX) testing whether agents notice problems unprompted — a complement to APEX's single-turn deliverable framing.

title: Toward an Evaluation Science for Generative AI Systems
why: Weidinger et al.'s position paper on rigorous GenAI evaluation that APEX cites; ties into the HELM / holistic-eval methodology cluster.

title: Instruction Tuning for Large Language Models: A Survey
why: High-influence survey of instruction tuning — the training side of the instruction-following capability IFEval measures.

title: The BiGGen Bench: A Principled Benchmark for Fine-grained Evaluation of Language Models with Language Models
why: Fine-grained, instance-specific rubric benchmark (Prometheus team) — links IFEval to the rubric / LLM-judge cluster (Prometheus, RaR, PRBench).

title: Self-play with Execution Feedback: Improving Instruction-following Capabilities of Large Language Models
why: AutoIF turns IFEval's 'verifiable instruction' idea into a training signal via code-verified execution feedback — its verifiable-reward descendant.

title: Improving Instruction-Following in Language Models through Activation Steering
why: Steers instruction-following via activation vectors from instruction pairs — a mechanistic complement to IFEval's behavioral measurement.

title: AGENTIF: Benchmarking Instruction Following of Large Language Models in Agentic Scenarios
why: Carries IFEval-style verifiable instruction-following into long, tool-using agent trajectories — instruction adherence under real agentic constraints.

title: A Survey on Evaluation of Large Language Models
why: The canonical survey of LLM evaluation — what to evaluate, where, and how; a hub the whole Benchmarks & Evals cluster points back to.

title: Large Language Models Are Not Robust Multiple Choice Selectors
why: The MCQ-bias paper that directly motivates MMLU-Pro's design: models have option-position/letter biases, so 4-option scores are unstable and gameable.

title: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards
why: Shows leaderboard rankings flip under trivial perturbations (answer-order, scoring) — the prompt-sensitivity problem MMLU-Pro set out to reduce.

title: SciBench: Evaluating College-Level Scientific Problem-Solving Abilities of Large Language Models
why: A source benchmark folded into MMLU-Pro; college-exam science requiring multi-step derivations — a reasoning-heavy eval in its own right.

title: Qwen Technical Report
why: The Qwen base/chat model report (3.9k cites); a foundational open-model family reported across the graph's eval and RL nodes.

title: Teaching Machines to Read and Comprehend
why: The CNN/Daily Mail cloze dataset -- the large-scale, semi-synthetic RC benchmark SQuAD was built to improve on; a cornerstone of the reading-comprehension lineage.

title: Adversarial Examples for Evaluating Reading Comprehension Systems
why: Showed SQuAD readers collapse on inserted distractor sentences -- the canonical demonstration that the benchmark is partly solved by surface lexical cues; a landmark eval-robustness paper.

title: Bidirectional Attention Flow for Machine Comprehension
why: BiDAF -- the canonical attention-based SQuAD reader; one of the architectures that drove the 2016-2018 leaderboard race and a direct citer of SQuAD.

title: Towards AI-Complete Question Answering: A Set of Prerequisite Toy Tasks
why: The bAbI tasks -- 20 synthetic challenges that stratify QA by reasoning type; an influential pre-SQuAD benchmark-design template.

title: The Goldilocks Principle: Reading Children's Books with Explicit Memory Representations
why: The Children's Book Test -- a large cloze RC dataset SQuAD contrasts against; central to its scale-vs-quality argument.

title: UI-TARS: Pioneering Automated GUI Interaction with Native Agents
why: End-to-end-trained native GUI agent (screenshots in, actions out) — a leading example of training computer-use agents rather than just scaffolding them.

title: Reinforced Self-Training (ReST) for Language Modeling
why: The grow-then-improve self-training loop that underpins offline RL-from-rewards recipes like Agent-RLVR.

title: SWE-RL: Advancing LLM Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning on Open Software Evolution
why: RL on 11M software-evolution instances — the direct large-scale counterpoint to Agent-RLVR's 817-environment recipe.

title: WebRL: Training LLM Web Agents via Self-Evolving Online Curriculum Reinforcement Learning
why: Self-evolving online-curriculum RL for web agents — the web-agent analogue of training SWE agents with environment rewards.

title: SWE-Fixer: Training Open-Source LLMs for Effective and Efficient GitHub Issue Resolution
why: Open-source retrieval+edit pipeline for issue resolution; the 30.2% SWE-Bench Verified baseline Agent-RLVR compares against.

title: Commit0: Library Generation from Scratch
why: Pushes SWE-agent evaluation beyond issue patching to generating whole libraries from scratch against unit tests.

title: Safety Alignment Should Be Made More Than Just a Few Tokens Deep
why: The 'shallow alignment' result the roadmap leans on: safety behavior lives in the first few tokens, so single-turn defenses are brittle and fine-tuning easily strips them.

title: Constitutional Classifiers: Defending against Universal Jailbreaks across Thousands of Hours of Red Teaming
why: Anthropic's output/input classifier defense, hardened over thousands of hours of red teaming — a concrete instance of the system-level safeguard stack the roadmap argues for.

title: Great, Now Write an Article About That: The Crescendo Multi-Turn LLM Jailbreak Attack
why: Canonical multi-turn jailbreak; central to the roadmap's claim that multi-turn attacks should be prioritized over single-turn.

title: ShieldGemma: Generative AI Content Moderation Based on Gemma
why: Google's open content-moderation classifier (cf. Llama Guard) — a deployable safeguard the roadmap's product/system layers depend on.

title: An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks
why: The frontier-risk taxonomy the roadmap cites to motivate why a single catastrophic failure can outweigh many minor policy violations.

title: Right for the Wrong Reasons: Diagnosing Syntactic Heuristics in Natural Language Inference
why: The foundational shortcut-learning diagnostic (HANS): NLI models that exploit lexical-overlap heuristics rather than entailment — the spurious-correlation precedent this paper extends from classification to generation.

title: COLLIE: Systematic Construction of Constrained Text Generation Tasks
why: The constrained text-generation benchmark the paper's instruction-following tasks are built on; a compositional constraint-satisfaction eval (sibling to IFEval).

title: A Long Way to Go: Investigating Length Correlations in RLHF
why: Same-author (Singhal) study of length over-optimization in RLHF — the canonical spurious shortcut in preference learning that motivates this work.

title: QuAC: Question Answering in Context
why: The conversational document-grounded QA dataset supplying the paper's docQA passages; a foundational context-dependent QA benchmark.

title: The Clever Hans Mirage: A Comprehensive Survey on Spurious Correlations in Machine Learning
why: A comprehensive survey of spurious correlations across ML that cites this paper; the broad taxonomy and mitigation landscape framing the FA/DN injection design.

title: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels
why: HyDE — the zero-shot query-expansion baseline REBEL benchmarks against: generate a hypothetical answer with an LLM, embed it, and retrieve on that embedding.

title: Passage Re-ranking with BERT
why: The paper that launched neural (BERT cross-encoder) reranking — the relevance-only reranking lineage REBEL extends with secondary criteria.

title: The use of MMR, diversity-based reranking for reordering documents and producing summaries
why: Maximal Marginal Relevance — the classical multi-criteria/diversity reranking method REBEL explicitly cites as its pre-LLM ancestor.

title: ROUGE: A Package for Automatic Evaluation of Summaries
why: The foundational summarization-overlap metric REBEL rejects in favor of rubric-based answer similarity; widely cited across the eval cluster.

title: ClassEval: A Manually-Crafted Benchmark for Evaluating LLMs on Class-level Code Generation
why: The class-level code-generation benchmark in MultiFileTest's single-file lineage; the step between function-level HumanEval and multi-file testing.

title: RepoCoder: Repository-Level Code Completion Through Iterative Retrieval and Generation
why: The canonical retrieval+generation approach to repository-level code; the cross-file context problem MultiFileTest deliberately keeps small to isolate.

title: SWT-Bench: Testing and Validating Real-World Bug-Fixes with Code Agents
why: Reproduces SWE-bench bug-fixes as test-generation tasks for code agents; the bug-reproduction sibling of MultiFileTest's whole-project test generation.

title: TestGenEval: A Real World Unit Test Generation and Test Completion Benchmark
why: Meta's single-file unit-test generation + completion benchmark; the immediate predecessor MultiFileTest extends from single-file to multi-file.

title: Prompting Large Language Models to Tackle the Full Software Development Lifecycle: DevBench
why: DevBench, the only prior benchmark that touched multi-file unit testing; MultiFileTest's headline comparison (3.75x more multi-file targets, enforced cross-file dependencies).

title: WildTeaming at Scale: From In-the-Wild Jailbreaks to (Adversarially) Safer Language Models
why: Mines in-the-wild jailbreaks at scale into adversarial training data and safer models; a high-cite red-teaming method in BrowserART's reference list.

title: Dissecting Adversarial Robustness of Multimodal LM Agents
why: A BrowserART reference probing adversarial attacks that make multimodal web agents fail; the agent-robustness counterpart to BrowserART's refusal-failure study.

title: WASP: Benchmarking Web Agent Security Against Prompt Injection Attacks
why: Benchmarks web-agent security against prompt injection — the injection-attack complement to BrowserART's refusal-failure axis; cites BrowserART.

title: ST-WebAgentBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Safety and Trustworthiness in Web Agents
why: A safety-and-trustworthiness benchmark for web agents (policy compliance, not just task success); a direct sibling that cites BrowserART.

title: OS-Harm: A Benchmark for Measuring Safety of Computer Use Agents
why: Extends agent-safety red-teaming from the browser to full computer-use agents (deliberate misuse, prompt injection, model mistakes); cites BrowserART.

title: WizardLM: Empowering Large Language Models to Follow Complex Instructions
why: Evol-Instruct, the instruction-evolution recipe that 'New Question Evolution' builds on; foundational synthetic-instruction method.

title: Textbooks Are All You Need
why: phi-1: the canonical case that synthetic-data quality, not just quantity, drives small-model performance.

title: Scaling Data-Constrained Language Models
why: The data-constrained scaling law this paper adapts to model per-query accuracy decay (R^2 > 0.98 fits).

title: Smaller, Weaker, Yet Better: Training LLM Reasoners via Compute-Optimal Sampling
why: Closest sibling work: compute-optimal choice of teacher/sampler for synthetic data — the same cost-effectiveness question on the teacher-strength axis.

title: RL on Incorrect Synthetic Data Scales the Efficiency of LLM Math Reasoning by Eight-Fold
why: Setlur et al.: training on incorrect synthetic data still helps — the basis for this paper's 'verification gives no measurable gain' finding.

title: Does Fine-Tuning LLMs on New Knowledge Encourage Hallucinations?
why: Central to the new-knowledge section: does finetuning on new facts induce hallucination? Directly cited.

title: When Scaling Meets LLM Finetuning: The Effect of Data, Model and Finetuning Method
why: Companion finetuning scaling-law study (data x model x method); complements the power-law claim.

title: The Unlocking Spell on Base LLMs: Rethinking Alignment via In-Context Learning
why: The in-context-learning (tuning-free) side of the 'is alignment superficial' debate; URIAL baseline.

title: Scaling Laws for Downstream Task Performance of Large Language Models
why: Scaling laws for downstream task performance under finetuning/transfer; sharpens the power-law framing.

title: Operationalising the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis via Task Complexity
why: Direct follow-up that operationalizes the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis via task complexity.

title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters
why: The inference-time-compute scaling thesis PLANSEARCH operationalizes for code; the Snell et al. result that motivated the search-over-samples line.

title: An Empirical Analysis of Compute-Optimal Inference for Problem-Solving with Language Models
why: Compute-optimal inference scaling for problem solving; sits beside Snell in the test-time-compute lineage PLANSEARCH extends.

title: Are More LLM Calls All You Need? Towards Scaling Laws of Compound Inference Systems
why: Scaling laws for compound inference systems (how many LLM calls help) - the cost/benefit framing of inference-time search PLANSEARCH lives in.

title: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning is a Policy Improvement Operator
why: Frames CoT as policy improvement (self-improvement loop); by PLANSEARCH last author Hugh Zhang, links CoT to the search-and-train agenda.

title: LMSYS-Chat-1M: A Large-Scale Real-World LLM Conversation Dataset
why: 1M real-world LLM conversations from the LMSYS group; the data substrate behind Chatbot Arena and a staple for eval/contamination work.

title: Proving Test Set Contamination in Black Box Language Models
why: A provable statistical test for benchmark contamination in black-box LLMs; anchors the atlas's contamination cluster that motivates live evaluation.

title: Rethinking Benchmark and Contamination for Language Models with Rephrased Samples
authors: Yang, Chiang, Zheng, Stoica, et al.
why: Shows rephrased benchmark items evade n-gram decontamination; the leakage threat that motivates fresh, live benchmarks like Arena.

title: ToxicChat: Unveiling Hidden Challenges of Toxicity Detection in Real-World User-AI Conversation
authors: Lin, Mao, Wang, Hu, Gui, et al.
why: Toxicity benchmark built from real user-AI conversations; Arena's safety-eval counterpart and a complement to the red-teaming cluster.

title: OpenAssistant Conversations - Democratizing Large Language Model Alignment
why: Large crowd-sourced assistant-conversation and preference dataset; a human-preference data counterpart to Chatbot Arena.

title: Contrastive Learning as Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
why: The contrastive goal-conditioned RL framework that the goal-conditioned reward-model objective is built on.

title: Is DPO Superior to PPO for LLM Alignment? A Comprehensive Study
why: Directly weighs DPO vs PPO for alignment; pairs with this paper's finding that PPO+contrastive RM generalizes better OOD than DPO.

title: Hindsight Experience Replay
why: Foundational goal-conditioned RL method; conceptual ancestor of learning representations toward goal states.

title: OpenMathInstruct-1: A 1.8 Million Math Instruction Tuning Dataset
why: The 1.8M GSM8K/MATH solution dataset used to build the reward-model preference pairs; anchors the math-reasoning data cluster.

title: HelpSteer: Multi-attribute Helpfulness Dataset for SteerLM
why: Multi-attribute (helpful/correct/coherent/complex) dataset used to build the steering prototype; links steerable-alignment work.

title: Who's Harry Potter? Approximate Unlearning in LLMs
why: Flagship LLM-unlearning demo (erasing Harry Potter knowledge); the approximate-unlearning counterpart to WMDP's RMU.

title: Knowledge Unlearning for Mitigating Privacy Risks in Language Models
why: Foundational gradient-ascent LLM unlearning for privacy; a direct methodological ancestor WMDP benchmarks unlearning against.

title: Shadow Alignment: The Ease of Subverting Safely-Aligned Language Models
why: A few benign-looking finetuning steps strip safety alignment — the threat model behind WMDP's finding that finetuning relearns unlearned hazards.

title: GPT-4 Is Too Smart To Be Safe: Stealthy Chat with LLMs via Cipher
why: Cipher-based jailbreak that bypasses safety training; red-teaming counterpart to WMDP's hazardous-knowledge elicitation.

title: Large Language Model Unlearning
why: Core gradient-ascent LLM-unlearning method; part of the unlearning-baseline lineage WMDP's RMU is measured against.

title: Targeted Latent Adversarial Training Improves Robustness to Persistent Harmful Behaviors in LLMs
why: The LAT defense MHJ evaluates (humans 75.4% ASR vs 10.0% automated ensemble).

title: Refuse Whenever You Feel Unsafe: Improving Safety in LLMs via Decoupled Refusal Training
why: The DERTA defense MHJ evaluates — pushes refusal training deeper into completions, yet still falls to 87.9% multi-turn human ASR.

title: Multi-Turn Context Jailbreak Attack on Large Language Models From First Principles
why: A concurrent *automated* multi-turn jailbreak attack — the kind of baseline that would disentangle 'multi-turn' from 'human' in MHJ's headline gap.

title: Eight Methods to Evaluate Robust Unlearning in LLMs
why: Directly relevant to MHJ's finding that RMU unlearning is recoverable — a survey of how to actually test whether knowledge is removed vs obfuscated.

title: Tamper-Resistant Safeguards for Open-Weight LLMs
why: CAIS/Scale-adjacent work on safeguards meant to survive adversarial finetuning — the robustness goal MHJ shows current defenses miss.

title: Generalization Analogies: A Testbed for Generalizing AI Oversight to Hard-To-Measure Domains
why: Concurrent work on reward-model/oversight generalization under distribution shift — the closest neighbor to this paper.

title: On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
why: Foundational calibration / ECE + temperature scaling paper underpinning the calibration framing used across eval work.

title: Can You Trust Your Model's Uncertainty? Evaluating Predictive Uncertainty Under Dataset Shift
why: Foundational study of predictive uncertainty + calibration degradation under dataset shift (Ovadia et al., NeurIPS 2019) — the classification result this paper extends to reward models.

title: WildBench: Benchmarking LLMs with Challenging Tasks from Real Users in the Wild
why: The closest sibling to Arena-Hard-Auto — an LLM-as-judge benchmark distilled from real WildChat user logs (the same source as Wild-Hard-Auto), with length-penalized scoring; Arena-Hard cites it and OpenRubrics evaluates on it.

title: Replacing Judges with Juries: Evaluating LLM Generations with a Panel of Diverse Models
why: Panel-of-LLM-evaluators (PoLL) — replaces one large judge with a jury of smaller diverse models to cut cost and intra-model bias; the direct precursor to Arena-Hard's Ensemble-as-Judges and a sibling of the judging-the-judges cluster.

title: MixEval: Deriving Wisdom of the Crowd from LLM Benchmark Mixtures
why: A contemporary 'cheap proxy for human preference' method — mixes existing ground-truth benchmarks to reach 0.96 correlation with Chatbot Arena; Arena-Hard cites and contrasts it as a live-benchmark alternative.

title: Extracting Training Data from Large Language Models
why: Carlini et al.'s foundational demonstration that LLMs memorize and regurgitate training data — the canonical citation behind the benchmark-contamination/leakage concern that motivates live, continuously-curated benchmarks like Arena-Hard.

title: A Broad-Coverage Challenge Corpus for Sentence Understanding through Inference
why: MNLI — GLUE's largest, most genre-diverse NLI task and the standard multi-genre entailment corpus; the diagnostic suite is scored through each model's MNLI classifier.

title: A large annotated corpus for learning natural language inference
why: SNLI — the 570k-pair corpus that made supervised NLI a benchmark task and the auxiliary data GLUE recommends for MNLI; foundational entailment dataset.

title: The Natural Language Decathlon: Multitask Learning as Question Answering
why: decaNLP — the contemporaneous 10-task benchmark GLUE positions against, recasting every task as question answering; the rival framing of general NLU evaluation.

title: SentEval: An Evaluation Toolkit for Universal Sentence Representations
why: SentEval — the sentence-encoder evaluation suite GLUE supersedes by dropping the encoder-only constraint; the direct methodological predecessor.

title: Neural Network Acceptability Judgments
why: CoLA — the grammatical-acceptability corpus that is GLUE's hardest single task (Matthews corr 8-35 for 2018 baselines); a probe of pure syntactic competence.

title: XLNet: Generalized Autoregressive Pretraining for Language Understanding
why: The autoregressive-permutation pretraining model that pushed GLUE's score past the human baseline (88.4) — the system whose saturation in Figure 1 motivated SuperGLUE.

title: BoolQ: Exploring the Surprising Difficulty of Natural Yes/No Questions
venue: North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics
why: A SuperGLUE constituent task: yes/no QA from naturally-occurring Google queries that proved surprisingly hard, exposing reading-comprehension gaps.

title: Task Contamination: Language Models May Not Be Few-Shot Anymore
why: Argues few-shot gains are confounded by pretraining-data contamination of benchmark test sets — the leakage problem static NLU leaderboards like SuperGLUE never audited.

title: Lessons from the Trenches on Reproducible Evaluation of Language Models
