why: The lm-evaluation-harness paper: how subtle prompt/formatting choices make LLM benchmark numbers irreproducible — practical lessons for any leaderboard.

title: Findings of the BabyLM Challenge: Sample-Efficient Pretraining on Developmentally Plausible Corpora
venue: Proceedings of the BabyLM Challenge at the 27th Conference on Computational Natural Language Learning
why: The BabyLM Challenge: sample-efficient pretraining under a fixed small data budget — the modern continuation of SuperGLUE's low-data / sample-efficiency emphasis.

title: The Stack: 3 TB of permissively licensed source code
why: The BigCode permissively-licensed code corpus underpinning SantaCoder/StarCoder — the pretraining-data foundation behind the code-gen models DS-1000 benchmarks, and a hub many existing code nodes cite.

title: LEVER: Learning to Verify Language-to-Code Generation with Execution
why: Trains a verifier on execution results to rerank code samples — the natural methodological successor to DS-1000's reliable execution-based evaluation, evaluated on Spider/MBPP/GSM8k.

title: Spider 2.0: Evaluating Language Models on Real-World Enterprise Text-to-SQL Workflows
why: The enterprise-scale successor to Spider: real database workflows where frontier models score far below human — a modern execution-graded data/code benchmark in DS-1000's lineage (Spider 1.0 already queued).

title: OctoPack: Instruction Tuning Code Large Language Models
why: Introduces CommitPack and HumanEvalPack (6 languages × fix/explain/synthesize) — extends execution-based code evaluation beyond Python single-task, a multilingual companion to DS-1000.

title: Magicoder: Empowering Code Generation with OSS-Instruct
why: OSS-Instruct generates diverse instruction data from open-source code snippets, training small models to rival much larger ones on HumanEval/MBPP/DS-1000 — an influential code instruction-tuning recipe.

title: DIN-SQL: Decomposed In-Context Learning of Text-to-SQL with Self-Correction
why: The decomposed in-context prompting method that posts BIRD's strongest result (GPT-4+DIN-SQL = 55.90% test EX); the canonical schema-linking → decomposition → self-correction pipeline for text-to-SQL.

title: Text-to-SQL Empowered by Large Language Models: A Benchmark Evaluation (DAIL-SQL)
why: The systematic benchmark study of LLM prompting strategies for text-to-SQL (question representation, example selection, supervised vs in-context); the methodological companion to BIRD/Spider.

title: CHASE-SQL: Multi-Path Reasoning and Preference Optimized Candidate Selection in Text-to-SQL
why: The modern agentic text-to-SQL pipeline (multi-path candidate generation + a fine-tuned selection agent) that topped the BIRD leaderboard, quantifying how far past GPT-4's 55% the field has moved.

title: Evaluating the Text-to-SQL Capabilities of Large Language Models
why: The first systematic zero/few-shot evaluation of LLMs (Codex) on Spider-style text-to-SQL; the prompting-baseline reference BIRD builds its ICL setup on (ref [39]).

title: Seq2SQL: Generating Structured Queries from Natural Language using Reinforcement Learning (WikiSQL)
why: WikiSQL — the foundational large-scale single-table text-to-SQL benchmark BIRD positions against as the clean-schema predecessor; the origin of execution-accuracy evaluation for SQL.

title: GSM-Plus: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating the Robustness of LLMs as Mathematical Problem Solvers
why: The direct parent dataset of QuestBench's GSM-Q: perturbs/adds distractors to GSM8K to probe math-reasoning robustness — the corpus QuestBench withholds variables from to build underspecified problems.

title: CLAMBER: A Benchmark of Identifying and Clarifying Ambiguous Information Needs
why: A benchmark for detecting and clarifying ambiguous information needs — the semantic-ambiguity counterpart to QuestBench's underspecification framing.

title: Ask-before-Plan: Proactive Language Agents for Real-World Planning
why: Proactive agents that decide when to ask before acting — connects QuestBench's Planning-Q clarification setting to agentic, embodied planning.

title: Semantic Uncertainty: Linguistic Invariances for Uncertainty Estimation in Natural Language Generation
why: Clusters semantically-equivalent generations to estimate a model's uncertainty — QuestBench cites it to separate semantic ambiguity from the underspecification it targets, and it underpins the 'detect when you don't know' failure mode.

title: Eliciting Human Preferences with Language Models
why: Generative active task elicitation: an LM asks questions to infer a user's latent preferences — the subjective-clarification line QuestBench contrasts against its objectively-scorable underspecification setting.

title: MathTutorBench: A Benchmark for Measuring Open-ended Pedagogical Capabilities of LLM Tutors
why: The direct successor by MathDial's own lead author: a benchmark that scores open-ended pedagogical ability of LLM tutors (Socratic guidance, scaffolding, error correction) — the modern evaluation layer over the MathDial/MRBench/TutorBench tutoring cluster.

title: AutoTutor meets Large Language Models: A Language Model Tutor with Rich Pedagogy and Guardrails
why: By a MathDial co-author: revives the classic AutoTutor with an LLM backbone plus pedagogical guardrails against answer-leaking — a concrete system built on the scaffolding-not-telling principle MathDial operationalizes.

title: CLASS: A Design Framework for Building Intelligent Tutoring Systems Based on Learning Science Principles
why: A learning-science-grounded framework for LLM intelligent tutoring systems (scaffolding + helpfulness), well-cited in the same tutoring line as MathDial; useful counterpoint on building vs evaluating tutors.

title: Can Language Models Solve Olympiad Programming? (USACO)
why: USACO: a contamination-aware olympiad-level competitive-programming benchmark where frontier models score in the single digits zero-shot — a hard code-reasoning eval extending the Atlas's benchmark cluster beyond GSM8K-grade difficulty.

title: Mathematical Capabilities of ChatGPT
why: An early systematic eval (the GHOSTS/miniGHOSTS datasets) of ChatGPT's mathematical ability across problem types — the 'models can solve but the picture is uneven' evidence MathDial cites to motivate that solving ≠ teaching.

title: SWAG: A Large-Scale Adversarial Dataset for Grounded Commonsense Inference
why: The direct predecessor — introduced Adversarial Filtering and the four-way commonsense sentence-completion format; the dataset BERT 'solved' via artifacts, which HellaSwag rebuilds to be robust.

title: Annotation Artifacts in Natural Language Inference Data
authors: Gururangan, Swayamdipta, Levy, Schwartz, Bowman, Smith
why: The artifact diagnosis HellaSwag is built around — NLI models exploit class-conditional cues in human-written hypotheses; Adversarial Filtering exists to strip exactly this leakage.

title: Adversarial NLI: A New Benchmark for Natural Language Understanding
authors: Nie, Williams, Dinan, Bansal, Weston, Kiela
why: Human-and-model-in-the-loop adversarial benchmark over three rounds — the most direct operationalization of HellaSwag's thesis that benchmarks must co-evolve with the state of the art.

title: Hypothesis Only Baselines in Natural Language Inference
authors: Poliak, Naradowsky, Haldar, Rudinger, Van Durme
why: Shows NLI is partly solvable from the hypothesis alone — the same answer-leakage HellaSwag's 'Ending Only' ablation exposes in SWAG; foundational evidence for why adversarial data collection is needed.

title: ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate
why: Multi-agent-debate LLM evaluators — a high-cite prior judge approach Prometheus 2 positions against; extends the LLM-as-judge cluster (MT-Bench, mr-bench, judging-the-judges).

title: LLM-Blender: Ensembling Large Language Models with Pairwise Ranking and Generative Fusion
why: Introduces PairRanker/PairRM — the pairwise-ranking baseline Prometheus 2 beats on PairRM's own in-domain HHH set; a foundational pairwise-evaluation node.

title: FLASK: Fine-grained Language Model Evaluation based on Alignment Skill Sets
why: The fine-grained, skill-set evaluation benchmark used (with human scores) as Prometheus 2's key human-correlation test; directly grounds the 0.555-vs-0.679 human-gap result.

title: From Generation to Judgment: Opportunities and Challenges of LLM-as-a-judge
why: The definitive survey of LLM-as-a-judge (525 cites) — a high-connectivity hub node that would tie together the whole judge/eval cluster in the Atlas.

title: JudgeBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-based Judges
why: A challenging meta-evaluation benchmark for LLM judges on hard reasoning/coding pairs — the modern stress-test of judges like Prometheus 2; pairs with thakur-judging-the-judges.

title: MMMU-Pro: A More Robust Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding Benchmark
why: The robustified follow-up to MMMU — adds a vision-only input setting, expands to 10 options, and filters text-answerable items; the direct next node and a pointed answer to MMMU's biggest critique (text-solvability).

title: MMBench: Is Your Multi-modal Model an All-around Player?
why: A foundational all-around multimodal benchmark (~2.2K cites) with CircularEval option-order robustness; a high-degree neighbor MMMU references and positions against on breadth and rigor.

title: MM-Vet: Evaluating Large Multimodal Models for Integrated Capabilities
why: Evaluates integrated multimodal capabilities via an LLM judge over 16 capability combinations; a widely-cited eval MMMU references and a bridge from the multimodal cluster to LLM-as-judge.

title: SEED-Bench: Benchmarking Multimodal LLMs with Generative Comprehension
why: 19K multiple-choice multimodal questions across 12 evaluation dimensions; a major contemporaneous LMM benchmark MMMU compares against on scale and answer format.

title: ChartQA: A Benchmark for Question Answering about Charts with Visual and Logical Reasoning
why: Foundational chart-reasoning VQA benchmark and a MathVista source dataset; the canonical chart-QA eval the multimodal-math lineage builds on.

title: BLIP-2: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training with Frozen Image Encoders and Large Language Models
why: The frozen-encoder + Q-Former VLM pretraining recipe (InstructBLIP's base) that defined efficient image-to-LLM alignment; backbone of several open LMMs MathVista evaluates.

title: Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning
why: The gated-cross-attention VLM that established few-shot multimodal in-context learning; ancestor of IDEFICS/OpenFlamingo, several of the LMMs MathVista benchmarks.

title: InstructBLIP: Towards General-purpose Vision-Language Models with Instruction Tuning
why: Instruction-tuned BLIP-2; one of the seven open-source LMMs evaluated in MathVista (InstructBLIP-Vicuna-7B), directly on the open-model frontier.

title: Seeing Things from a Different Angle: Discovering Diverse Perspectives about Claims
authors: Sihao Chen, Daniel Khashabi, Wenpeng Yin, Chris Callison-Burch, Dan Roth
why: The formal claim/perspective stance benchmark DEBAGREEMENT is directly compared against (90.5% in-domain vs sub-majority transfer to Reddit); covering it connects the formal-vs-messy stance lineage.

title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
why: ViT — the patch-as-token image backbone underneath modern multimodal LLMs; cited by ScienceQA and the multimodal-eval lineage (MathVista/MMMU).

title: CLEVR: A Diagnostic Dataset for Compositional Language and Elementary Visual Reasoning
why: The diagnostic compositional visual-reasoning benchmark with controlled question types; a template for probing multimodal reasoning failures.

title: IconQA: A New Benchmark for Abstract Diagram Understanding and Visual Language Reasoning
why: Pan Lu's abstract-diagram visual-reasoning benchmark, a direct sibling/predecessor of ScienceQA in the same multimodal-reasoning line.

title: Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks
why: The siamese sentence-embedding model behind the semantic-similarity metric ScienceQA uses to score generated explanations; a workhorse eval/retrieval primitive.

title: SummEval: Re-evaluating Summarization Evaluation
why: The summary-level meta-evaluation benchmark (CNN/DM, 4 aspects) that G-Eval and UniEval are scored on; foundational for summarization-eval correlation.

title: BARTScore: Evaluating Generated Text as Text Generation
why: Generation-likelihood evaluator (BART) and a core G-Eval baseline; the strongest pre-LLM reference-based metric on SummEval/QAGS.

title: Towards a Unified Multi-Dimensional Evaluator for Text Generation
why: UniEval — the trained T5 multi-dimensional evaluator that was SOTA before G-Eval and the direct reference-free comparison G-Eval dethrones.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models for News Summarization
why: The Zhang et al. 2023 human-vs-GPT-3.5 summary study whose data G-Eval reuses for its self-preference analysis (alpha=0.07 annotator agreement).

title: Evaluating the Factual Consistency of Abstractive Text Summarization
why: FactCC — BERT-based factual-consistency classifier; a baseline in G-Eval QAGS hallucination evaluation and an anchor of the summarization-faithfulness lineage.

title: XNLI: Evaluating Cross-lingual Sentence Representations
why: Foundational cross-lingual NLI benchmark (15 languages) built by translating the test set; the prototype of the 'multilingual on the surface only' eval MultiLoKo critiques.

title: The Belebele Benchmark: a Parallel Reading Comprehension Dataset in 122 Language Variants
why: 122-language parallel reading-comprehension benchmark (built on Flores); a current multilingual workhorse and a direct foil for MultiLoKo's locally-sourced, non-parallel design.

title: TyDi QA: A Benchmark for Information-Seeking Question Answering in Typologically Diverse Languages
why: Information-seeking QA in 11 typologically-diverse languages, separately sourced per language; the closest prior 'native, not translated' QA benchmark MultiLoKo positions against.

title: The Flores-101 Evaluation Benchmark for Low-Resource and Multilingual Machine Translation
why: Many-to-many low-resource MT evaluation across 101 languages; the parallel-data backbone Belebele and much multilingual eval are built on, and a reference for MultiLoKo's translation analysis.

title: Global MMLU: Understanding and Addressing Cultural and Linguistic Biases in Multilingual Evaluation
why: Shows translated MMLU carries cultural/linguistic bias and that many items need local knowledge; the translated-benchmark distortion MultiLoKo formalizes as its Locality Effect.

title: BLEURT: Learning Robust Metrics for Text Generation
why: A BERT-based learned metric pre-trained on synthetic perturbations then fine-tuned on human ratings; the model-based successor that reframes 'closeness to a reference' as a learned function.

title: COMET: A Neural Framework for MT Evaluation
why: The SOTA learned, source-aware MT metric trained on human judgments that largely displaced BLEU on WMT leaderboards; the modern answer to what BLEU could not measure.

title: chrF: character n-gram F-score for automatic MT evaluation
why: Character-n-gram F-score that adds recall and handles morphology better than word-level BLEU; a standard reported metric (with sacreBLEU) and co-cited with BLEU across the atlas (e.g. MultiLoKo).

title: Re-evaluating the Role of Bleu in Machine Translation Research
why: The canonical 'BLEU considered harmful' critique — shows BLEU can rank a worse system above a better one and that real improvements need not raise BLEU; the natural counterpoint node to the BLEU explainer's eval-rigor read.

title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
why: The dominant PEFT method; low-rank adapters benchmarked here and the backbone of modern LLM fine-tuning.

title: Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP
why: The original adapter-based PEFT method that launched parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

title: BitFit: Simple Parameter-efficient Fine-tuning for Transformer-based Masked Language-models
why: Bias-only fine-tuning; one of the four PEFT methods this paper benchmarks.

title: Few-Shot Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning is Better and Cheaper than In-Context Learning
why: Introduces (IA)³ / T-Few, the most memory-frugal method in this benchmark.

title: Revisiting Parameter-Efficient Tuning: Are We Really There Yet?
why: The prior PEFT evaluation (on RoBERTa) this paper explicitly extends to billion-parameter LLMs.

title: Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation Quality Using N-gram Co-occurrence Statistics
why: The NIST metric METEOR benchmarks against (Table 1) — a weighted-n-gram refinement of BLEU and a foundational node in the automatic-MT-metric lineage.

title: The Significance of Recall in Automatic Metrics for MT Evaluation
why: METEOR's own precursor establishing that recall matters more than precision for metric/human correlation — the empirical basis for METEOR's recall-weighted Fmean.

title: Meteor Universal: Language Specific Translation Evaluation for Any Target Language
why: The trained, multilingual successor that directly fixes this paper's hand-tuned-parameters and English-only-synonymy limitations with learned per-language weights.

title: A Call for Clarity in Reporting BLEU Scores
why: SacreBLEU — shows BLEU scores are irreproducible across tokenization/normalization choices and standardizes reporting; essential context for the BLEU/METEOR comparison.

title: MoverScore: Text Generation Evaluating with Contextualized Embeddings and Earth Mover Distance
why: Embedding-based metric (contextualized BERT embeddings + Earth Mover's Distance) — the soft-matching successor that breaks METEOR's surface-lexical ceiling, alongside BERTScore.

title: Leveraging Passage Retrieval with Generative Models for Open Domain Question Answering
why: Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD): the canonical generative successor to DPR — feeds DPR-retrieved passages into a seq2seq reader, long the SOTA open-domain QA recipe

title: Poly-encoders: Architectures and Pre-training Strategies for Fast and Accurate Multi-sentence Scoring
why: Maps the dual-encoder vs cross-encoder accuracy/latency tradeoff DPR sits on; the poly-encoder middle ground

title: Learning to Retrieve Reasoning Paths over Wikipedia Graph for Question Answering
why: Graph-based multi-hop retrieval baseline DPR is measured against; the multi-hop counterpoint to single-shot dense retrieval

title: Real-Time Open-Domain Question Answering with Dense-Sparse Phrase Index
authors: Seo, Lee, Kwiatkowski, Parikh, Farhadi, Hajishirzi
why: DenSPI — phrase-indexed retrieval that skips passage reading entirely; the alternative retrieval paradigm DPR contrasts with

title: Can LLMs Express Their Uncertainty? An Empirical Evaluation of Confidence Elicitation in LLMs
why: Benchmarks confidence elicitation across LLMs - directly motivates LAB-Bench's accuracy/precision/coverage split and the 'decline to answer' option for reliable science tools.

title: Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools
why: ChemCrow: the seminal LLM science agent that plans and executes chemistry tasks with external tools - the tool-augmented direction LAB-Bench explicitly leaves to the community.

title: Autonomous chemical research with large language models
why: Coscientist: an end-to-end autonomous chemistry agent; LAB-Bench adapted its 0-shot chain-of-thought evaluation strategy for scoring models.

title: Performance of ChatGPT on USMLE: Potential for AI-Assisted Medical Education Using Large Language Models
why: Landmark medical-LLM evaluation (ChatGPT near passing USMLE); a high-impact domain-specific eval that pairs with the Med-PaLM clinical-knowledge node.

title: PaperQA: Retrieval-Augmented Generative Agent for Scientific Research
why: Same-authors (FutureHouse) precursor that introduced LitQA and the RAG agent LAB-Bench cites as outperforming tool-free LLMs on literature QA.

title: Understanding Contrastive Representation Learning through Alignment and Uniformity on the Hypersphere
why: The alignment/uniformity diagnostic SimCSE (and the contrastive-embedding field) uses to measure representation quality.

title: SemEval-2017 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity Multilingual and Crosslingual Focused Evaluation
why: The STS-B benchmark that anchors SimCSE's headline metric and the whole sentence-embedding eval line.

title: Supervised Learning of Universal Sentence Representations from Natural Language Inference Data
why: InferSent: the NLI-supervised sentence-embedding baseline whose recipe SimCSE reframes as contrastive learning.

title: Skip-Thought Vectors
why: Early self-supervised sentence embeddings (predict surrounding sentences) -- the unsupervised baseline SimCSE's dropout objective supersedes.

title: Whitening Sentence Representations for Better Semantics and Faster Retrieval
why: BERT-whitening: the post-processing isotropy baseline SimCSE directly compares against (and beats) on STS.

title: LongBench: A Bilingual, Multitask Benchmark for Long Context Understanding
why: Direct long-context successor to LRA: a bilingual, multitask benchmark for evaluating LLM long-context understanding on real tasks.

title: RULER: What's the Real Context Size of Your Long-Context Language Models?
why: Synthetic long-context benchmark that stress-tests claimed context windows (retrieval, multi-hop, aggregation) - the modern Path-X in spirit.

title: Efficient Transformers: A Survey
why: The canonical taxonomy of the xformer model class LRA evaluates; same lead author, a natural reference hub for the efficiency cluster.

title: Rethinking Attention with Performers
why: FAVOR+ kernel attention; the best Pathfinder performer in LRA and one of its ten benchmarked models; also cited by FlashAttention.

title: Reformer: The Efficient Transformer
why: LSH + reversible-layer attention; an LRA model (best on ListOps) and a landmark efficient-Transformer architecture.

title: Are Emergent Abilities in Large Language Models just In-Context Learning?
why: The other deflationary account: argues most claimed emergent abilities reduce to in-context learning plus instruction tuning, not a new capability appearing at scale -- a complementary, mechanism-level companion to the metric-artifact argument.

title: Understanding Emergent Abilities of Language Models from the Loss Perspective
why: The strongest rebuttal: shows emergent abilities persist when models are compared by pre-training loss rather than parameter/compute scale, and appear even on continuous metrics below a loss threshold -- directly contesting the 'mirage' framing.

title: A Theory for Emergence of Complex Skills in Language Models
why: Arora & Goyal's 'skill-mix' theory derives, from scaling laws, why competence at compositions of skills should emerge super-linearly -- a principled case that some emergence is real, not measurement artifact.

title: Broken Neural Scaling Laws
why: A smoothly-broken power law that fits and extrapolates non-monotonic, sharply-transitioning scaling curves; cited by Mirage as the view that emergence is real (a change in the governing power law), the foil to its single-power-law account.

title: The Quantization Model of Neural Scaling
why: Posits discrete 'quanta' of knowledge learned in order of frequency, explaining both smooth aggregate scaling and abrupt per-quantum emergence; cited by Mirage as a model under which emergence can be genuine.

title: Making the V in VQA Matter: Elevating the Role of Image Understanding in Visual Question Answering
why: VQA v2 -- the balanced-pairs successor that directly fixes the language-prior shortcut VQA exposed: every question is paired with two images giving different answers, forcing the model to use the image.

title: GQA: A New Dataset for Real-World Visual Reasoning and Compositional Question Answering
why: Scene-graph-grounded compositional VQA with controlled answer distributions and new consistency/validity metrics -- the reasoning-focused, bias-controlled answer to VQA's shortcut critique.

title: OK-VQA: A Visual Question Answering Benchmark Requiring External Knowledge
why: Pushes VQA beyond the image: questions need outside knowledge no pixels supply -- the knowledge/retrieval branch of the VQA lineage.

title: CIDEr: Consensus-based Image Description Evaluation
why: Consensus-based caption-evaluation metric by two VQA authors; slots into the automatic-text-metric lineage (BLEU, METEOR) already in the graph and underpins VQA's claim that captioning eval was unsolved.

title: Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context
authors: Lin, Maire, Belongie, Hays, Perona, Ramanan, Dollar, Zitnick
why: The object-detection/segmentation/captioning dataset VQA's real images and the whole multimodal-eval cluster (ScienceQA, MathVista, MMMU) are built on -- a foundational anchor node.
